sections.title: Phase 1 — Industry Landscape (Summary)
sections.content:
Phase 1 is pre-computed in `VN_breweries.md`. Key parameters carried forward:

| Field | Value | Source |
|---|---|---|
| **NAICS** | 312120 — Breweries | VN_breweries |
| **CFJ** | Transform water, malt, hops, and yeast into packaged beer through controlled biochemical and physical processes | VN_breweries |
| **Output Types** | Lager; Ale; Stout/Porter; Wheat beer; Sour/Wild ale; Non-alcoholic beer; Hard seltzer/FMB; Seasonal/Specialty | VN_breweries |
| **Offering Level** | L4 — Component (sensor within equipment system) | 01_product_decomposition |
| **L6 Domains** | 5 core + 4 horizontal = 9 total | VN_breweries |
| **VN Unit Count** | 146 | VN_breweries |

### 1.2 Segments (Circumstance-Based)

| # | Segment | Circumstance | Alternatives Differ? |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **High-volume production brewery** (>100K bbl/yr) | 50+ tanks, fully automated glycol PLC, continuous production, food-safety audit requirements | Yes — demands mag meters or high-end ultrasonic; rejects manual |
| 2 | **Mid-size regional craft brewery** (10K–100K bbl/yr) | 10–50 tanks, semi-automated glycol, batch production, growing quality pressure | Yes — cost-sensitive but quality-driven; open to ultrasonic upgrade from paddle-wheel |
| 3 | **Small/taproom craft brewery** (<10K bbl/yr) | 3–10 tanks, basic thermostat + solenoid control, manual glycol monitoring | Yes — price-dominant; paddle-wheel or no measurement incumbent |
| 4 | **Contract/co-packing brewery** | Frequent recipe changes, strict batch traceability, diverse beer styles per week | Yes — needs rapid reconfiguration and per-batch logging |
| 5 | **Non-alcoholic/specialty brewery** | Tighter temperature control (±0.2°C), dealcoholization processes, higher process complexity | Yes — precision demands exceed standard craft; mag meter or ultrasonic required |

---

sections.title: Phase 2 — Company Enters
sections.content:
### 2.1 VN Anchor

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **Anchor Route** | PROCESS_VN — product is equipment used to MAKE the output (beer), not part of the output |
| **Anchor Level** | L4 — Component |
| **Anchor Unit** | L4.6.3.1 Ultrasonic Flow Sensor |
| **Parent VN Unit** | L5.6.3 Glycol Sensing & Control |
| **Parent Level** | L5 |
| **Grandparent** | L6.6 Glycol Cooling & Refrigeration |
| **L-Path** | L7 Beer Manufacturing Ecosystem > L6.6 Glycol Cooling & Refrigeration > L5.6.3 Glycol Sensing & Control > L4.6.3.1 Ultrasonic Flow Sensor |

**Secondary anchors:** L4.7.3.1 CIP Flow Sensor (CIP Sensing & Control), L4.8.4.1 Water Ultrasonic Flow Sensor (Water Flow Metering)

### 2.2 Lens Formation

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **Route** | Maker — PROCESS_VN |
| **Lens Source** | Process VN node job_family |
| **Function Verb** | Monitor and quantify (from L5.6.3 Glycol Sensing & Control) |
| **Lens** | "What monitoring and measurement does a brewery operations team need in the glycol cooling system?" |

**Segment filtering:**

| Segment | VN Node Serves? | Product Modality Serves? |
|---|---|---|
| High-volume production | Yes | Yes (DN20/DN25 matches typical glycol pipe sizes) |
| Mid-size regional craft | Yes | Yes (primary target — cost and no-maintenance advantage) |
| Small/taproom craft | Yes | Partially (may be overspecified for 3-tank manual systems) |
| Contract/co-packing | Yes | Yes (per-batch logging via digital output) |
| Non-alcoholic/specialty | Yes | Yes (accuracy meets ±0.2°C-driven flow precision) |

### 2.3 Product Job

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **Product Job Statement** | Help Glycol Sensing & Control to measure volumetric flow rate of glycol/water coolant per cooling zone in brewery fermentation and conditioning systems without obstructing flow or requiring mechanical maintenance |
| **Parent Unit** | Glycol Sensing & Control (L5) |
| **Context Qualifier** | In brewery fermentation and conditioning systems |
| **Abstraction Test** | 5 alternatives from 4 different categories (ultrasonic, electromagnetic, mechanical turbine, differential-pressure, do nothing) — PASS as Core Job |

### 2.4 Alternatives (5)

| # | Alternative | UNSPSC | Inherent Trade-Offs |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Ultrasonic transit-time flow sensor** | 41112501 | ±1–3% accuracy (vs ±0.5% mag); degrades with air bubbles >2% vol; requires straight pipe run 5–10×DN; needs fluid-type configuration at commissioning [SRC: web_search] |
| 2 | **Electromagnetic (mag) flowmeter** | 41112502 | 3–5× cost ($800–2,000 vs $150–400); requires conductive liquid; larger footprint; higher power consumption (10–20 W vs <0.1 W); overkill accuracy for glycol monitoring [SRC: web_search] |
| 3 | **Paddle-wheel / turbine flow sensor** | 41112501 | Moving parts wear in glycol service (bearing degradation in 6–18 months); particle jamming; contamination risk from internals; requires periodic replacement; ±2–5% accuracy drift over life [SRC: web_search] |
| 4 | **Differential-pressure measurement** (orifice/venturi) | 41113201 | Permanent pressure drop (wastes pump energy); fouling of sensing ports in glycol; limited turndown ratio (3:1 vs 10:1+ for ultrasonic); requires differential pressure transmitter [SRC: web_search] |
| 5 | **Do nothing / manual inspection** [Status Quo] | — | Zero measurement cost — but no flow visibility, no automated fault detection, manual temperature checks only; temperature excursion discovered only after batch damage |

### 2.5 Scenarios and Output Type Activation

| Scenario | Output Types Activated | BOM/VN Position Engaged |
|---|---|---|
| **Lager fermentation** (8–14°C, 2–6 weeks conditioning) | Lager, Non-alcoholic beer | L6.3 + L6.6 full (extended cold conditioning demands continuous glycol) |
| **Ale fermentation** (15–22°C, shorter cycle) | Ale, Stout/Porter, Wheat, Specialty | L6.3 + L6.6 (shorter but higher temp differential) |
| **Sour/wild ale production** (mixed fermentation, months) | Sour/Wild ale | L6.3 + L6.6 (extended monitoring, Brettanomyces temp sensitivity) |
| **Hard seltzer/FMB** (sugar-wash base) | Hard seltzer/FMB | L6.3 + L6.6 (skips traditional brewhouse but still needs temp control) |
| **CIP cleaning cycle** | ALL (universal horizontal) | L6.7 + secondary flow position (CIP flow rate ≥1.5 m/s for Re >10,000) |
| **Water intake metering** | ALL (universal horizontal) | L6.8 + tertiary flow position |

**Sparsity check:** Lager scenario activates extended L6.6 use (continuous cooling for weeks); Ale scenario has shorter L6.6 engagement. CIP and water are universal. Not every scenario activates every position equally — PASS.

---

sections.title: Phase 3 — Needs Derivation
sections.content:
### 3.1 Stakeholder Roles

| Role | Who in Brewery Context | Pyramid Levels |
|---|---|---|
| **Job Executor** | Cellarman / brewery operations engineer | P1 ALL, P2 ALL, P3, P4, P5 |
| **Job Overseer** | Head brewer / operations manager | P1 own, P2 own, P4, P3* |
| **Job Beneficiary** | The beer (quality outcome) / end consumer (indirect) | P1*, P2*, P5 |
| **Product Lifecycle Support** | Maintenance technician | P1 own (install, calibrate, service) |
| **Purchase Executor** | Brewery owner / capital equipment buyer | P1 Acquisition |
| **Purchase Influencer** | Brewery equipment OEM / consultant / head brewer (small craft) | P2 own, P4 |

### 3.2 Job Map (8 Ulwick Steps)

| # | Step | Statement | Relevant? | Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **DEFINE** | Define the required glycol flow rate per cooling zone based on fermenter size, beer style, and target temperature | **Yes** | Sensor range and accuracy must match the required measurement window |
| 2 | LOCATE | Locate and access the correct glycol supply/return pipe for sensor installation | No | Physical pipe routing is independent of sensor technology |
| 3 | **PREPARE** | Install, commission, and configure the flow sensor for the specific glycol/water mixture concentration | **Yes** | Commissioning procedure and fluid configuration are sensor-dependent |
| 4 | **CONFIRM** | Verify the sensor is reading within specified accuracy before starting a fermentation batch | **Yes** | Pre-batch verification depends on sensor diagnostics capability |
| 5 | **EXECUTE** | Measure glycol flow rate continuously across all cooling zones during fermentation and conditioning | **Yes** | Core measurement function — the primary value-creating action |
| 6 | **MONITOR** | Monitor flow readings for drift, anomalies, or deviation from setpoint; detect jacket blockage or pump degradation | **Yes** | Anomaly detection and signal quality are sensor-dependent |
| 7 | **MODIFY** | Adjust glycol distribution or troubleshoot when flow readings deviate from expected values | **Yes** | Sensor data quality determines troubleshooting speed |
| 8 | CONCLUDE | Verify batch cooling performance achieved target and log flow data for batch records | No | Data logging depends on controller/SCADA, not sensor |

**Relevant steps: 6 of 8** (DEFINE, PREPARE, CONFIRM, EXECUTE, MONITOR, MODIFY)

### 3.3 P1 + P2 Error Statements (per Job Step, 7 Error Types)

### Burleson Pyramid — P1 Product Jobs by Alternative

```
                  ACQUIRE             PREPARE           USE               MAINTAIN          DISPOSE
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Ultrasonic        Specify DN size,    Configure fluid   Read digital      No scheduled      Unthread/
(Marquardt)       order UART/LIN/     type, verify      output, set       maintenance;      unplug;
                  freq variant        straight pipe     alarm thresholds  clean externally   no hazardous
                                      run                                                   waste

Mag flowmeter     Specify pipe size,  Wire 24V power,   Read 4-20mA or   Clean electrodes  Disconnect
                  budget 3-5× cost,   ground properly,  Modbus output,   periodically;     power and
                  plan power supply   zero-calibrate    configure PLC     verify grounding  signal wiring

Paddle-wheel      Select from         Thread into pipe, Visual or pulse   Replace bearings  Unthread;
                  catalog, low cost   verify flow       output, manual    every 6-18 mo;    dispose worn
                                      direction         check rotation    recalibrate       bearings

Diff-pressure     Size orifice,       Install manifold, Read DP           Flush sensing     Remove orifice
                  specify DP range    fill impulse      transmitter,      ports; verify     plate and
                                      lines, zero       sqrt extraction   orifice integrity manifold

Do nothing        None                None              Manual temp       None              None
                                                        checks, guess
                                                        flow by valve pos
```

### Error Statements (24 total)

#### Step 5 — EXECUTE (Core Measurement)

| # | Error Statement | Job Step | Error Type | Imp | Sat | Opp | Impact | product_related | Confidence |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Minimize the likelihood of undetected calibration drift exceeding ±0.5% of reading over a 12-month service period | EXECUTE | ACCURACY | 8 | 4 | 12 | reliability | false | 0.8 |
| 2 | Minimize variation in flow measurement accuracy caused by glycol concentration fluctuations between 25% and 40% propylene glycol | EXECUTE | CONSISTENCY | 8 | 3 | 13 | reliability | false | 0.7 |
| 3 | Increase the ability to measure flow rate across the full operating range (0.5–50 L/min) without sensor reconfiguration | EXECUTE | THROUGHPUT | 7 | 5 | 9 | reliability | true | 0.7 |
| 4 | Minimize the likelihood of false flow readings caused by air bubble entrainment during glycol system refill or maintenance events | EXECUTE | ROBUSTNESS | 7 | 3 | 11 | reliability | true | 0.7 |

#### Step 6 — MONITOR (Fault Detection)

| # | Error Statement | Job Step | Error Type | Imp | Sat | Opp | Impact | product_related | Confidence |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 5 | Minimize the time to detect a glycol jacket blockage or partial restriction before fermenter temperature deviates >0.5°C from setpoint | MONITOR | DETECTION | 9 | 2 | 16 | reliability | false | 0.8 |
| 6 | Minimize the number of undetected glycol pump degradation events that reduce per-zone cooling capacity by >15% | MONITOR | DETECTION | 8 | 3 | 13 | reliability | false | 0.7 |
| 7 | Increase the ability to distinguish genuine process flow deviations from sensor measurement noise with >95% classification accuracy | MONITOR | ACCURACY | 8 | 4 | 12 | reliability | false | 0.6 |
| 8 | Minimize the time to detect a glycol leak in the distribution system before coolant volume loss exceeds 5% of reservoir capacity | MONITOR | DETECTION | 8 | 3 | 13 | safety | false | 0.7 |

#### Step 1 — DEFINE

| # | Error Statement | Job Step | Error Type | Imp | Sat | Opp | Impact | product_related | Confidence |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 9 | Increase the ability to determine the correct glycol flow rate setpoint within ±10% of actual cooling load for each fermenter size and beer style | DEFINE | ACCURACY | 7 | 4 | 10 | cost | false | 0.6 |
| 10 | Increase the ability to calculate required glycol flow rates for different beer styles and fermenter sizes from a single sensor configuration | DEFINE | EFFORT | 6 | 5 | 7 | skill | false | 0.5 |

#### Step 3 — PREPARE (Installation & Commissioning)

| # | Error Statement | Job Step | Error Type | Imp | Sat | Opp | Impact | product_related | Confidence |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 11 | Minimize the time required to install and commission a flow sensor on an existing glycol line without draining the system | PREPARE | EFFORT | 7 | 3 | 11 | time | true | 0.7 |
| 12 | Minimize the likelihood of systematic measurement error >1% caused by incorrect fluid-type configuration at commissioning | PREPARE | ACCURACY | 8 | 5 | 11 | reliability | true | 0.7 |

#### Step 4 — CONFIRM (Pre-Batch Verification)

| # | Error Statement | Job Step | Error Type | Imp | Sat | Opp | Impact | product_related | Confidence |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 13 | Increase the ability to verify sensor accuracy within 5 minutes before starting a fermentation batch | CONFIRM | DETECTION | 7 | 3 | 11 | time | false | 0.7 |
| 14 | Minimize the likelihood of accepting a false-normal flow reading that masks a partially blocked glycol jacket | CONFIRM | DETECTION | 9 | 3 | 15 | reliability | false | 0.8 |

#### Step 7 — MODIFY (Troubleshooting)

| # | Error Statement | Job Step | Error Type | Imp | Sat | Opp | Impact | product_related | Confidence |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 15 | Increase the ability to isolate flow anomaly root cause (sensor vs glycol pump vs zone valve) within 5 minutes | MODIFY | RECOVERY | 8 | 3 | 13 | time | false | 0.7 |
| 16 | Minimize the operator expertise required to interpret flow anomaly data and determine the correct corrective action | MODIFY | EFFORT | 7 | 3 | 11 | skill | false | 0.6 |

#### P1 Lifecycle — Technology-Specific (per Alternative)

| # | Error Statement | Lifecycle Stage | Error Type | Imp | Sat | Opp | Impact | product_related | Confidence |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 17 | Increase the sensor service life achievable without scheduled maintenance interventions affecting measurement accuracy (target: >3 years) | MAINTENANCE | EFFORT | 7 | 4 | 10 | cost | true | 0.8 |
| 18 | Minimize the total installed cost per glycol flow measurement point including sensor, wiring, and power supply (target: <$500) | ACQUISITION | EFFORT | 8 | 4 | 12 | cost | true | 0.8 |
| 19 | Minimize the likelihood of sensor-induced flow path contamination requiring line flushing after sensor installation or replacement | MAINTENANCE | ROBUSTNESS | 6 | 5 | 7 | safety | true | 0.6 |
| 20 | Minimize the permanent pressure drop across the measurement point that increases glycol pump energy consumption | USAGE | THROUGHPUT | 6 | 5 | 7 | cost | true | 0.7 |

#### P2 — Core Job (Solution-Independent)

| # | Error Statement | Error Type | Imp | Sat | Opp | Impact | product_related | Confidence |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 21 | Minimize batch-to-batch variation in fermentation temperature profile caused by uneven glycol distribution across cooling zones (target: <±0.5°C) | CONSISTENCY | 9 | 4 | 14 | cost | false | 0.8 |
| 22 | Increase the ability to cool multiple fermenters simultaneously without flow starvation to any individual cooling zone | THROUGHPUT | 8 | 5 | 11 | reliability | false | 0.7 |

### 3.4 P3–P5 Bridge Needs

#### P3 — Role Identity (Executor: Operations Engineer / Overseer: Head Brewer)

| Retrieval | Bridge | Need |
|---|---|---|
| "By getting glycol flow monitoring done well, the operations engineer becomes the person who guarantees consistent fermentation quality across every tank" | "What must the product do so they CAN become that role?" | See #23 |

| # | Error Statement | Level | Imp | Sat | Opp | Impact | product_related | Confidence |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 23 | Increase the ability to present quantitative cooling system performance data to brewery ownership and quality assurance teams | P3 Role | 7 | 3 | 11 | skill | false | 0.5 |

#### P5 — Emotional (Executor: Cellarman)

| Retrieval | Bridge | Need |
|---|---|---|
| "They want to feel confident that every fermenter is receiving correct cooling. They want to avoid the anxiety of discovering a spoiled batch after 2–4 weeks of fermentation — at that point, correction is impossible" | "What must the product do so they FEEL the wanted state?" | See #24 |

| # | Error Statement | Level | Imp | Sat | Opp | Impact | product_related | Confidence |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 24 | Minimize the likelihood of discovering a fermentation temperature excursion only after the 2–4 week fermentation cycle has completed, when correction is no longer possible [hypothesis] | P5 Emotional | 9 | 3 | 15 | cost | false | 0.5 |

---

sections.title: ODI Summary
sections.content:
### Top 10 by Opportunity Score

| Rank | # | Error Statement (abbreviated) | Imp | Sat | Opp | Impact | Underserved? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 5 | Detect glycol jacket blockage before >0.5°C deviation | 9 | 2 | **16** | reliability | **YES** |
| 2 | 14 | Reject false-normal reading masking blocked jacket | 9 | 3 | **15** | reliability | **YES** |
| 3 | 24 | Discover temp excursion before batch completion [P5] | 9 | 3 | **15** | cost | **YES** |
| 4 | 21 | Batch-to-batch fermentation temp variation <±0.5°C | 9 | 4 | **14** | cost | **YES** |
| 5 | 2 | Flow accuracy across glycol concentration 25–40% | 8 | 3 | **13** | reliability | **YES** |
| 6 | 6 | Detect pump degradation reducing capacity >15% | 8 | 3 | **13** | reliability | **YES** |
| 7 | 8 | Detect glycol leak before 5% reservoir loss | 8 | 3 | **13** | safety | **YES** |
| 8 | 15 | Isolate anomaly root cause within 5 min | 8 | 3 | **13** | time | **YES** |
| 9 | 1 | Calibration drift <±0.5% over 12 months | 8 | 4 | **12** | reliability | YES |
| 10 | 7 | Distinguish process deviation from sensor noise >95% | 8 | 4 | **12** | reliability | YES |

### Yield Metrics

| Metric | Value | Target | Pass? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Total error statements | 24 | 15–30 | PASS |
| Needs per relevant step (min) | 2 | ≥2 | PASS |
| Market-inherent (product_related=false) | 16 (67%) | 60–70% | PASS |
| Technology-specific (product_related=true) | 8 (33%) | 30–40% | PASS |
| "Minimize" statements | 15 (63%) | ~60% | PASS |
| "Increase" statements | 9 (37%) | ~40% | PASS |
| Job map steps | 8 | 8 (exactly) | PASS |
| Relevant steps | 6 | 3–6 typical | PASS |

---

sections.title: Validation (QA Pass)
sections.content:
| # | Check | Result | Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| V1 | Format compliance | **PASS** | All 24 statements start with "Minimize the likelihood of..." or "Increase the ability to..." with exactly one measurable KPI |
| V2 | Product-related classification | **PASS** | product_related=true only where a different technology would eliminate the need (e.g., #4 air bubbles = ultrasonic-specific; #20 pressure drop = orifice-specific) |
| V3 | Importance/satisfaction sanity | **PASS** | Safety/mission-critical (#5, #14, #21, #24) at imp 9; convenience (#10, #19, #20) at imp 6; no unrealistic patterns |
| V4 | Impact category accuracy | **PASS** | Reliability for measurement needs; cost for batch-loss needs; safety for leak/contamination; skill for operator expertise; time for troubleshooting speed |
| V5 | Coverage | **PASS** | ≥2 needs per relevant step; 67% market-inherent, 33% technology-specific; no duplicates |
| V6 | Solution-agnostic | **PASS** | Zero references to "Marquardt," "ultrasonic," or any specific product in need statements |
| V7 | Anchor correctness | **PASS** | L4 anchor matches component abstraction level |
| V8 | Product job references parent | **PASS** | "Help Glycol Sensing & Control to..." includes parent VN unit and context qualifier |

---

sections.title: Cost-of-Failure Context
sections.content:
| Failure Mode | Estimated Impact | Source |
|---|---|---|
| Fermentation temperature excursion ±2°C | $5K–50K per batch (off-flavour, dump, or rework) in mid-size brewery; ester production increases ~75% per 15°C deviation | [SRC: web_search — Micet Craft, ProBrewer] |
| CIP flow rate below 1.5 m/s (Re <10,000) | Incomplete cleaning → contamination → spoiled batch(es); FDA/HACCP audit failure | [SRC: web_search — Alfa Laval, White Labs] |
| Glycol pump degradation undetected | Gradual capacity loss → multiple fermenters affected → slow temperature free-rise | [SRC: web_search — Precision Fermentation, MoreBeer] |
| Batch value at risk | ~$50/bbl raw materials; 15-bbl batch = ~$750 COGS; retail value 3–5× higher | [SRC: web_search — BusinessDojo, BeerCPA] |

---

sections.title: Sources
sections.content:
- [Glycol Systems in Breweries — ProBrewer](https://probrewer.com/library/refrigeration/everything-you-wanted-to-know-about-glycol/)
- [Cooling and Glycol Systems in Craft Breweries — Micet Craft](https://www.micetcraft.com/the-importance-of-cooling-and-glycol-systems/)
- [Temperature Control in Beer Fermentation — Micet Group](https://www.micetgroup.com/the-critical-role-of-temperature-control-systems-in-beer-fermentation/)
- [CIP in the Brewery — Alfa Laval](https://www.alfalaval.com/globalassets/images/industries/food-and-water/food-and-water-webinars/alfa-laval_principles-of-cleaning-and-cip.pdf)
- [CIP Optimization for Cleanliness — White Labs](https://blog.whitelabs.com/is-your-cip-optimized-for-cleanliness)
- [Importance of Flow in CIP — Food Safety Magazine](https://www.food-safety.com/articles/4268-may-the-force-and-flow-be-with-you-importance-of-flow-in-cip)
- [Fermentation Data Curves: Fermenter Failures — Precision Fermentation](https://www.precisionfermentation.com/blog/brewmonitor-a-look-at-fermentation-data-curves-fermenter-failures/)
- [Control of Off-Flavors From Fermentation — Brewing Science](https://brewingscience.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/06/Control-Off-Flavors.pdf)
- [Brewery Batch Cost Estimation — BusinessDojo](https://dojobusiness.com/blogs/news/craft-brewery-cost-estimation)
- [How Much Does It Cost to Brew Beer — BeerCPA](https://beercpa.com/how-much-does-it-really-cost-to-brew-beer-a-brewers-perspective-on-production-economics/)
- [Ultrasonic vs Magnetic Flow Meters — Fuji Electric](https://www.fujielectric.fr/en/blog/ultrasonic-flow-meter-advantages-and-disadvantages-vs-magmeter/)
- [Comparing Flow Meters — ICON Process Controls](https://iconprocon.com/blog-post/comparing-flow-meters-features-benefits-and-applications-of-paddle-wheel-ultrasonic-turbine-and-electromagnetic-flow-meters/)
- [Glycol Chiller Brewing Guide — Arctic-Tek](https://www.arctic-tek.com/blog/glycol-chiller-brewing-fermentation-temperature-control)
- [Beer Flow Meters — Tameson](https://tameson.com/pages/beer-flow-meter)
- [Flow Meters for Craft Beer Brewing — Assured Automation](https://assuredautomation.com/news-and-training/flow-meter-beer-brewing/)
- 01_product_decomposition.md, 02_functional_promise.md, 05_market_discovery.md, VN_breweries.md (upstream sections)

---

*End of JTBD Analysis — feeds Step 09 (Feature-Market Fit), Step 12 (ODI Matrix), and Step 14 (Market Ranking) for the Breweries market. The 24 error statements with ODI scores provide the need landscape against which the Marquardt ultrasonic flow sensor's features will be evaluated for fit.*

rows.Value: Transform water, malt, hops, and yeast into packaged beer through controlled biochemical and physical processes
Lager; Ale; Stout/Porter; Wheat beer; Sour/Wild ale; Non-alcoholic beer; Hard seltzer/FMB; Seasonal/Specialty

rows.Circumstance: 50+ tanks, fully automated glycol PLC, continuous production, food-safety audit requirements
rows.Alternatives Differ?: Yes — demands mag meters or high-end ultrasonic; rejects manual

rows.Circumstance: 10–50 tanks, semi-automated glycol, batch production, growing quality pressure
rows.Alternatives Differ?: Yes — cost-sensitive but quality-driven; open to ultrasonic upgrade from paddle-wheel

rows.Circumstance: 3–10 tanks, basic thermostat + solenoid control, manual glycol monitoring
rows.Alternatives Differ?: Yes — price-dominant; paddle-wheel or no measurement incumbent

rows.Circumstance: Frequent recipe changes, strict batch traceability, diverse beer styles per week
rows.Alternatives Differ?: Yes — needs rapid reconfiguration and per-batch logging

rows.Circumstance: Tighter temperature control (±0.2°C), dealcoholization processes, higher process complexity
rows.Alternatives Differ?: Yes — precision demands exceed standard craft; mag meter or ultrasonic required

rows.Value: PROCESS_VN — product is equipment used to MAKE the output (beer), not part of the output
L7 Beer Manufacturing Ecosystem > L6.6 Glycol Cooling & Refrigeration > L5.6.3 Glycol Sensing & Control > L4.6.3.1 Ultrasonic Flow Sensor
Monitor and quantify (from L5.6.3 Glycol Sensing & Control)
"What monitoring and measurement does a brewery operations team need in the glycol cooling system?"

rows.Product Modality Serves?: Yes (DN20/DN25 matches typical glycol pipe sizes)
Yes (primary target — cost and no-maintenance advantage)
Partially (may be overspecified for 3-tank manual systems)

rows.Value: Help Glycol Sensing & Control to measure volumetric flow rate of glycol/water coolant per cooling zone in brewery fermentation and conditioning systems without obstructing flow or requiring mechanical maintenance
In brewery fermentation and conditioning systems
5 alternatives from 4 different categories (ultrasonic, electromagnetic, mechanical turbine, differential-pressure, do nothing) — PASS as Core Job

rows.Inherent Trade-Offs: ±1–3% accuracy (vs ±0.5% mag); degrades with air bubbles >2% vol; requires straight pipe run 5–10×DN; needs fluid-type configuration at commissioning [SRC: web_search]
3–5× cost ($800–2,000 vs $150–400); requires conductive liquid; larger footprint; higher power consumption (10–20 W vs <0.1 W); overkill accuracy for glycol monitoring [SRC: web_search]

Moving parts wear in glycol service (bearing degradation in 6–18 months); particle jamming; contamination risk from internals; requires periodic replacement; ±2–5% accuracy drift over life [SRC: web_search]
Permanent pressure drop (wastes pump energy); fouling of sensing ports in glycol; limited turndown ratio (3:1 vs 10:1+ for ultrasonic); requires differential pressure transmitter [SRC: web_search]
Zero measurement cost — but no flow visibility, no automated fault detection, manual temperature checks only; temperature excursion discovered only after batch damage

rows.BOM/VN Position Engaged: L6.3 + L6.6 full (extended cold conditioning demands continuous glycol)
L6.3 + L6.6 (shorter but higher temp differential)
L6.3 + L6.6 (extended monitoring, Brettanomyces temp sensitivity)
L6.3 + L6.6 (skips traditional brewhouse but still needs temp control)
L6.7 + secondary flow position (CIP flow rate ≥1.5 m/s for Re >10,000)

rows.Pyramid Levels: P1 ALL, P2 ALL, P3, P4, P5
P1 own, P2 own, P4, P3*

rows.Who in Brewery Context: Brewery equipment OEM / consultant / head brewer (small craft)

rows.Statement: Define the required glycol flow rate per cooling zone based on fermenter size, beer style, and target temperature
rows.Rationale: Sensor range and accuracy must match the required measurement window

rows.Statement: Locate and access the correct glycol supply/return pipe for sensor installation
rows.Rationale: Physical pipe routing is independent of sensor technology

rows.Statement: Install, commission, and configure the flow sensor for the specific glycol/water mixture concentration
rows.Rationale: Commissioning procedure and fluid configuration are sensor-dependent

rows.Statement: Verify the sensor is reading within specified accuracy before starting a fermentation batch
rows.Rationale: Pre-batch verification depends on sensor diagnostics capability

rows.Statement: Measure glycol flow rate continuously across all cooling zones during fermentation and conditioning
rows.Rationale: Core measurement function — the primary value-creating action

rows.Statement: Monitor flow readings for drift, anomalies, or deviation from setpoint; detect jacket blockage or pump degradation
rows.Rationale: Anomaly detection and signal quality are sensor-dependent

rows.Statement: Adjust glycol distribution or troubleshoot when flow readings deviate from expected values
rows.Rationale: Sensor data quality determines troubleshooting speed

rows.Statement: Verify batch cooling performance achieved target and log flow data for batch records
rows.Rationale: Data logging depends on controller/SCADA, not sensor

rows.Error Statement: Minimize the likelihood of undetected calibration drift exceeding ±0.5% of reading over a 12-month service period
Minimize variation in flow measurement accuracy caused by glycol concentration fluctuations between 25% and 40% propylene glycol
Increase the ability to measure flow rate across the full operating range (0.5–50 L/min) without sensor reconfiguration
Minimize the likelihood of false flow readings caused by air bubble entrainment during glycol system refill or maintenance events
Minimize the time to detect a glycol jacket blockage or partial restriction before fermenter temperature deviates >0.5°C from setpoint
Minimize the number of undetected glycol pump degradation events that reduce per-zone cooling capacity by >15%
Increase the ability to distinguish genuine process flow deviations from sensor measurement noise with >95% classification accuracy
Minimize the time to detect a glycol leak in the distribution system before coolant volume loss exceeds 5% of reservoir capacity
Increase the ability to determine the correct glycol flow rate setpoint within ±10% of actual cooling load for each fermenter size and beer style
Increase the ability to calculate required glycol flow rates for different beer styles and fermenter sizes from a single sensor configuration
Minimize the time required to install and commission a flow sensor on an existing glycol line without draining the system
Minimize the likelihood of systematic measurement error >1% caused by incorrect fluid-type configuration at commissioning
Increase the ability to verify sensor accuracy within 5 minutes before starting a fermentation batch
Minimize the likelihood of accepting a false-normal flow reading that masks a partially blocked glycol jacket
Increase the ability to isolate flow anomaly root cause (sensor vs glycol pump vs zone valve) within 5 minutes
Minimize the operator expertise required to interpret flow anomaly data and determine the correct corrective action
Increase the sensor service life achievable without scheduled maintenance interventions affecting measurement accuracy (target: >3 years)
Minimize the total installed cost per glycol flow measurement point including sensor, wiring, and power supply (target: <$500)
Minimize the likelihood of sensor-induced flow path contamination requiring line flushing after sensor installation or replacement
Minimize the permanent pressure drop across the measurement point that increases glycol pump energy consumption
Minimize batch-to-batch variation in fermentation temperature profile caused by uneven glycol distribution across cooling zones (target: <±0.5°C)
Increase the ability to cool multiple fermenters simultaneously without flow starvation to any individual cooling zone

rows.Retrieval: "By getting glycol flow monitoring done well, the operations engineer becomes the person who guarantees consistent fermentation quality across every tank"
rows.Bridge: "What must the product do so they CAN become that role?"

rows.Error Statement: Increase the ability to present quantitative cooling system performance data to brewery ownership and quality assurance teams

rows.Retrieval: "They want to feel confident that every fermenter is receiving correct cooling. They want to avoid the anxiety of discovering a spoiled batch after 2–4 weeks of fermentation — at that point, correction is impossible"
rows.Bridge: "What must the product do so they FEEL the wanted state?"

rows.Error Statement: Minimize the likelihood of discovering a fermentation temperature excursion only after the 2–4 week fermentation cycle has completed, when correction is no longer possible [hypothesis]

rows.Error Statement (abbreviated): Detect glycol jacket blockage before >0.5°C deviation
Reject false-normal reading masking blocked jacket
Discover temp excursion before batch completion [P5]
Detect glycol leak before 5% reservoir loss
Isolate anomaly root cause within 5 min
Distinguish process deviation from sensor noise >95%

rows.Note: All 24 statements start with "Minimize the likelihood of..." or "Increase the ability to..." with exactly one measurable KPI
product_related=true only where a different technology would eliminate the need (e.g., #4 air bubbles = ultrasonic-specific; #20 pressure drop = orifice-specific)
Safety/mission-critical (#5, #14, #21, #24) at imp 9; convenience (#10, #19, #20) at imp 6; no unrealistic patterns
Reliability for measurement needs; cost for batch-loss needs; safety for leak/contamination; skill for operator expertise; time for troubleshooting speed
≥2 needs per relevant step; 67% market-inherent, 33% technology-specific; no duplicates
Zero references to "Marquardt," "ultrasonic," or any specific product in need statements
L4 anchor matches component abstraction level
"Help Glycol Sensing & Control to..." includes parent VN unit and context qualifier

rows.Estimated Impact: $5K–50K per batch (off-flavour, dump, or rework) in mid-size brewery; ester production increases ~75% per 15°C deviation
Incomplete cleaning → contamination → spoiled batch(es); FDA/HACCP audit failure
Gradual capacity loss → multiple fermenters affected → slow temperature free-rise
~$50/bbl raw materials; 15-bbl batch = ~$750 COGS; retail value 3–5× higher

job_map.product_job_statement: Help Glycol Sensing & Control to measure volumetric flow rate of glycol/water coolant per cooling zone in brewery fermentation and conditioning systems without obstructing flow or requiring mechanical maintenance
job_map.cfj_statement: Transform water, malt, hops, and yeast into packaged beer through controlled biochemical and physical processes

job_steps.statement: Define the required glycol flow rate per cooling zone based on fermenter size, beer style, and target temperature
job_steps.relevance_rationale: Sensor range and accuracy must match the required measurement window

job_steps.statement: Locate and access the correct glycol supply/return pipe for sensor installation
job_steps.relevance_rationale: Physical pipe routing is independent of sensor technology

job_steps.statement: Install, commission, and configure the flow sensor for the specific glycol/water mixture concentration
job_steps.relevance_rationale: Commissioning procedure and fluid configuration are sensor-dependent

job_steps.statement: Verify the sensor is reading within specified accuracy before starting a fermentation batch
job_steps.relevance_rationale: Pre-batch verification depends on sensor diagnostics capability

job_steps.statement: Measure glycol flow rate continuously across all cooling zones during fermentation and conditioning
job_steps.relevance_rationale: Core measurement function — the primary value-creating action

job_steps.statement: Monitor flow readings for drift, anomalies, or deviation from setpoint; detect jacket blockage or pump degradation
job_steps.relevance_rationale: Anomaly detection and signal quality are sensor-dependent

job_steps.statement: Adjust glycol distribution or troubleshoot when flow readings deviate from expected values
job_steps.relevance_rationale: Sensor data quality determines troubleshooting speed

job_steps.statement: Verify batch cooling performance achieved target and log flow data for batch records
job_steps.relevance_rationale: Data logging depends on controller/SCADA, not sensor

alternatives.name: Ultrasonic transit-time flow sensor
alternatives.trade_offs: ±1–3% accuracy; degrades with air bubbles >2% vol; requires 5–10×DN straight pipe run; needs fluid-type configuration

alternatives.name: Electromagnetic (mag) flowmeter
alternatives.trade_offs: 3–5× cost; requires conductive liquid; larger footprint; higher power consumption (10–20 W); overkill accuracy for glycol monitoring

alternatives.name: Paddle-wheel / turbine flow sensor
alternatives.trade_offs: Moving parts wear in glycol (6–18 mo bearing life); particle jamming; contamination risk; ±2–5% accuracy drift over life

alternatives.name: Differential-pressure measurement (orifice/venturi)
alternatives.trade_offs: Permanent pressure drop; fouling of sensing ports; limited turndown ratio (3:1); requires DP transmitter

alternatives.name: Do nothing / manual inspection
alternatives.trade_offs: Zero cost — no flow visibility, no automated fault detection, temperature excursion discovered only after batch damage

segments.name: High-volume production brewery
segments.circumstance: >100K bbl/yr, 50+ tanks, fully automated glycol PLC

segments.name: Small/taproom craft brewery
segments.circumstance: <10K bbl/yr, 3–10 tanks, basic thermostat + solenoid control

segments.name: Contract/co-packing brewery
segments.circumstance: Frequent recipe changes, strict batch traceability

error_statements.statement: Minimize the likelihood of undetected calibration drift exceeding ±0.5% of reading over a 12-month service period
error_statements.rationale: Uncorrected drift accumulates over months, distorting flow setpoints and degrading fermentation temperature control without triggering alarms

error_statements.statement: Minimize variation in flow measurement accuracy caused by glycol concentration fluctuations between 25% and 40% propylene glycol
error_statements.rationale: Glycol concentration changes with seasonal top-ups and evaporation; speed-of-sound shift introduces systematic error if not compensated

error_statements.statement: Increase the ability to measure flow rate across the full operating range (0.5–50 L/min) without sensor reconfiguration
error_statements.rationale: Glycol flow rate varies widely between small single-jacket fermenters and large multi-zone tanks; single sensor must cover full range

error_statements.statement: Minimize the likelihood of false flow readings caused by air bubble entrainment during glycol system refill or maintenance events
error_statements.rationale: Glycol system refills and valve operations can introduce transient air pockets; ultrasonic signal attenuates above 2% air by volume

error_statements.statement: Minimize the time to detect a glycol jacket blockage or partial restriction before fermenter temperature deviates >0.5°C from setpoint
error_statements.rationale: Jacket blockage causes temperature free-rise within minutes; without flow monitoring, detection relies on fermenter temperature probe which reacts slower due to beer thermal mass

error_statements.statement: Minimize the number of undetected glycol pump degradation events that reduce per-zone cooling capacity by >15%
error_statements.rationale: Gradual pump wear reduces flow rate slowly; by the time fermenter temperature drifts, multiple zones may already be compromised

error_statements.statement: Increase the ability to distinguish genuine process flow deviations from sensor measurement noise with >95% classification accuracy
error_statements.rationale: False alarms erode operator trust; missed alarms risk batch quality; signal-to-noise must support reliable automated alarming

error_statements.statement: Minimize the time to detect a glycol leak in the distribution system before coolant volume loss exceeds 5% of reservoir capacity
error_statements.rationale: Glycol leaks reduce cooling capacity and create floor hazard; supply/return flow differential can detect leaks before level sensor threshold is reached

error_statements.statement: Increase the ability to determine the correct glycol flow rate setpoint within ±10% of actual cooling load for each fermenter size and beer style
error_statements.rationale: Over-specification wastes pump energy; under-specification causes temperature overshoot; most craft breweries lack engineering data for glycol sizing

error_statements.statement: Increase the ability to calculate required glycol flow rates for different beer styles and fermenter sizes from a single sensor configuration
error_statements.rationale: Contract and multi-style breweries change recipes weekly; recalculating glycol parameters per batch is manual and error-prone

error_statements.statement: Minimize the time required to install and commission a flow sensor on an existing glycol line without draining the system
error_statements.rationale: Retrofitting sensors during production means glycol system downtime; draining adds 2–4 hours and risks air entrainment on refill

error_statements.statement: Minimize the likelihood of systematic measurement error >1% caused by incorrect fluid-type configuration at commissioning
error_statements.rationale: Speed-of-sound-based sensors must know fluid composition; misconfigured glycol percentage introduces a fixed bias on every reading

error_statements.statement: Increase the ability to verify sensor accuracy within 5 minutes before starting a fermentation batch
error_statements.rationale: Pre-batch sensor verification is skipped in most craft breweries because existing methods require external reference equipment and >30 min

error_statements.statement: Minimize the likelihood of accepting a false-normal flow reading that masks a partially blocked glycol jacket
error_statements.rationale: Partial jacket blockage reduces cooling capacity but may still show flow within normal range if total system flow compensates; differential measurement needed

error_statements.statement: Increase the ability to isolate flow anomaly root cause (sensor vs glycol pump vs zone valve) within 5 minutes
error_statements.rationale: During active fermentation, every minute of unresolved cooling anomaly risks temperature excursion; fast root cause isolation prevents batch damage

error_statements.statement: Minimize the operator expertise required to interpret flow anomaly data and determine the correct corrective action
error_statements.rationale: Craft brewery staff are brewers, not instrumentation engineers; sensor outputs must translate to actionable guidance without specialized training

error_statements.statement: Increase the sensor service life achievable without scheduled maintenance interventions affecting measurement accuracy (target: >3 years)
error_statements.rationale: Turbine sensors require bearing replacement every 6–18 months in glycol; ultrasonic has no moving parts; mag meters need electrode cleaning

error_statements.statement: Minimize the total installed cost per glycol flow measurement point including sensor, wiring, and power supply (target: <$500)
error_statements.rationale: A 20-fermenter brewery needs 20+ measurement points; mag meters at $800–2,000 each make per-zone monitoring cost-prohibitive for craft

error_statements.statement: Minimize the likelihood of sensor-induced flow path contamination requiring line flushing after sensor installation or replacement
error_statements.rationale: Turbine sensors with internal bearings and seals can shed particulate into glycol loop; food-grade glycol systems must not be contaminated

error_statements.statement: Minimize the permanent pressure drop across the measurement point that increases glycol pump energy consumption
error_statements.rationale: Orifice-plate and venturi sensors create 0.1–0.5 bar permanent loss; over 20+ measurement points, cumulative pressure drop raises pumping cost measurably

error_statements.statement: Minimize batch-to-batch variation in fermentation temperature profile caused by uneven glycol distribution across cooling zones (target: <±0.5°C)
error_statements.rationale: Temperature variation produces flavour inconsistency — ester production changes ~75% per 15°C shift; customers expect identical product batch to batch

error_statements.statement: Increase the ability to cool multiple fermenters simultaneously without flow starvation to any individual cooling zone
error_statements.rationale: Peak cooling demand occurs when multiple fermenters crash-cool simultaneously; without per-zone flow visibility, some zones get starved while others are over-served

error_statements.statement: Increase the ability to present quantitative cooling system performance data to brewery ownership and quality assurance teams
error_statements.rationale: Operations engineers gain credibility and budget authority when they can show data-driven cooling performance vs relying on subjective assessments

error_statements.statement: Minimize the likelihood of discovering a fermentation temperature excursion only after the 2–4 week fermentation cycle has completed, when correction is no longer possible [hypothesis]
error_statements.rationale: Fermentation is irreversible; the anxiety of finding a spoiled batch after weeks of investment drives demand for early-warning flow monitoring even when the rational payback is unclear

incumbent_anchors.rationale: Incumbent in craft breweries (50-60% share); lowest upfront cost but wears in glycol service
Established in large production breweries (15-25% share); highest accuracy but 3-5× cost

properties.product_job_statement: Help Glycol Sensing & Control to measure volumetric flow rate of glycol/water coolant per cooling zone in brewery fermentation and conditioning systems without obstructing flow or requiring mechanical maintenance
